focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 9

Under the western overhang, last night's coals had gone gummy with rain. Fasa crouched at the fire pit while the work bell carried thinly across the compound, breaking the crust apart with a stick and feeding dried grass into the damp center until a thin flame caught. Across twenty naming cycles she had learned that the naming told her what people were willing to declare, but the kitchen told her what their hands knew.

The cooking area was a stone ledge on the compound's western side, open to the courtyard and the smell of wet hillside beyond. The air beneath the overhang was layered — ash at the bottom, green mineral rain above, and the persistent wood-smoke that had worked its way into everyone's hair and clothing by the end of the first week. The wet season made everything slow. Even fire needed coaxing.

Suna Mera arrived first among the assigned families, carrying the morning's water in a clay jug balanced on her hip. She set it down without greeting, but the absence of greeting was itself familiar by now — they had reached the stage of proximity where not every arrival required acknowledgment. Deshi followed a few paces behind, her posture evaluative before she'd even looked at the cooking area, the lean angle of her shoulders conveying judgment about things she hadn't yet seen.

"The coals are still damp," Deshi said. She knelt beside the fire pit and held her palm over the heat, testing. "You need harder wood underneath. Softwood burns wet."

Fasa did not point out that she had been building fires at this shrine for two decades. She moved the clay jug to the flat stone where the cutting board lived and began slicing onions from the garden. Twenty years had taught her when to assert authority and when to let a woman who needed control find it. The k...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...f a life that hadn't required you to sit in a stone compound and watch your father lose to a man who didn't bother to win.

She thought about her journal. She'd write tonight, same as every night — on her mat, oil lamp turned low so her mother wouldn't tell her to sleep, her pen moving across the page in handwriting that got worse as the dark got deeper. She'd been recording the days with the scrupulous attention she gave things when she didn't know the point yet but didn't trust herself to miss one. The details of the naming, who said what, who sat where, the arguments and their outcomes. But tonight the part she'd write first wasn't Olet's sentence. It was Pik under the overhang, saying his family back to himself until it sounded rehearsed. It was Suna sorting and re-sorting the damp biscuits. It was her mother watching the storeroom while Ada swallowed.

Her father had been right in every way you could write on a board with arrows between the points. It hadn't bought him anything she could touch. Not Kael's stopped hand. Not Pik's careful recitation. Not the way her mother kept looking at the kitchen door while the baby drank. Being right hadn't opened a road or a cupboard. It had just taught everyone how to brace.

The scary part was how far tomorrow had already gotten into their bodies. Into Pik's careful recitation, into Kael backing off when Suna covered the towel, into Nessa watching the storeroom latch while her baby ate. Bram had given the trap a clean name. The rest of them were already learning its shape by feel.

Below them the flood made its constant sound — the steady rush of water over a road that no one could use. Tomas threw his last stone and it hit the wall dead center and fell straight down.

The bell would ring again tomorrow, and they'd answer it.
